rulings.Case Title: JOHN POPPIN VS. GEORGE V CRESSON et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Peter M. Radin, Jr.S Motion For Order Releasing Judgment Liens
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Wednesday, January 22, 2025, line 1, DEFENDANT PETER RADIN'S Motion For Order Releasing Judgment Liens Defendant Peter Radin, Jr.'s motion for order releasing judgment liens is denied. Mr. Radin has submitted insufficient proof that plaintiff John Poppin is deceased and, failing such proof, has an obligation to personally serve Mr. Poppin with this motion. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. = (302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: FORBES TATE PARTNERS, LLC VS. DONE GLOBAL, INC.
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Vacate Sister State Judgment
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Wednesday, January 22, 2025, line 2, DEFENDANT DONE GLOBAL, INC.'S Motion To Vacate Sister State Judgment Defendant Done Global. Inc.'s motion to vacate sister state judgment is granted. Done Global has established its entitlement to vacation of this court's judgment on the District of Columbia Superior Court's judgment per Code of Civil Procedure 1710.40. This motion is not untimely. While the motion was not filed until more than 30 days after the notice of appearance of counsel for Done Global and that notice did not specify that the appearance was only a limited one to contest jurisdiction of this court, under the circumstances of this case the notice of appearance is properly construed as a special appearance limited to contesting jurisdiction and thus did not trigger the 30-day time frame to file a motion to vacate the sister state judgment. Done Global has also shown that service on Ms. He of the sister state judgment was not proper service on Done Global because, at the time of the service on Ms. He, she was not an officer or general manager of Done Global. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. rulings.Case Title: ABRAHIM KARADSHE VS. SHERIN SAYED MOHAMED ABDELWAHAB ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION To Set Aside Default And Dismiss Case, And Memo Of Points And Authorities
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Wednesday, January 22, 2025, line 11, DEFENDANT SHERIN ABDELWAHAB MOTION To Set Aside Default And Dismiss Case Defendant Abdelwahab's motion to vacate the default/default judgment is denied as moot. Plaintiff's January 3, 2025, declaration indicates that he resolved this matter with defendant and defendant has no knowledge of this motion. Plaintiff filed an acknowledgment of satisfaction of judgment on June 10, 2024, which renders this motion moot. It makes no sense for the defendant to be moving to vacate the default judgment when the judgment has been satisfied. The court notes that the signature on the November 25, 2024 Abdelwahab declaration looks nothing like the signature on the underlying Artist and Promoter Agreement, which is attached as exhibit A to the complaint. rulings.Case Title: SHIVANI PATEL VS. 1625 LEAVENWORTH STREET, LLC, A CALIFORNIA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Protective Order Re: Shivani Patel Deposition, Written Discovery Requests And Motions To Compel; Request For Sanctions Against Attorney Thomas Purtell In The Amount Of $7,1775.75; Code Of Civ. Proc. 2023.030(A), 2023.010
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Wednesday, January 22, 2025, line 1, DEFENDANT 1625 LEAVENWORTH STREET, LLC, A CALIFORNIA LIMITED LIABILITY COMPANY, VICTOR MAKRAS, VICTOR MAKRAS DOING BUSINESS AS MAKRAS REAL ESTATE'S Motion For Protective Order Re: Shivani Patel Deposition, Written Discovery Requests And Motions To Compel; Request For Sanctions Against Attorney Thomas Purtell In The Amount Of $7,1775.75; Code Of Civ. Proc. 2023.030(A), 2023.010 (part 1 tentative ruling, see part 2) Defendant 1625 Leavenworth Street, LLC, Victor Makras, and Victor Makras, Inc.'s motion for a protective order is denied. Defendants request five forms of relief, labeled (a)-(e) in their notice of motion. Three of these requests are moot or seek relief that should be addressed to another department of this court. Requests (c) and (d) are moot. The court ruled upon Plaintiff's motions to compel further discovery on January 16, 2025 and awarded the appropriate relief. Further relief here would be misplaced. Request (e) seeks relief that should be addressed to department 501, not this department. On April 25, 2024 Judge Haines of department 501 granted Defendants' motion to consolidate this case with Westnedge for trial purposes only. Any motion seeking reconsideration or further consolidation should be directed to department 501. Defendants also request in (e) that the court enter a "protective order preventing plaintiffs' counsel from serving duplicative discovery in the each Westnedge and Patel actions." (Notice 2:23-24.) But Defendants fail to address this point in their memorandum or reply. Defendants' two remaining requests fail on the merits. Request (a) seeks a protective order requiring Plaintiff's counsel to cease obstructing deposition testimony to allow for full and fair cross examination of Plaintiff. Request (b) seeks an order compelling Plaintiff to be produced for deposition no later than December 31, 2024. Defendants argue they are entitled to these protective orders under CCP 2025.420. That statute allows the court to protect a party or deponent from "unwarranted annoyance, embarrassment, or oppression, or undue burden and expense." (Subd. (b.) Defendants fail to show they are entitled to relief under CCP 2025.420. Defendants' citations to excerpts of Ms. Patel's deposition fall short of establishing that Plaintiff's counsel abused the discovery process by preventing Defendants from obtaining material testimony that justifies another deposition. Because defendants' positions on this motion did not lack substantial justification, plaintiff's request for monetary sanctions is denied. (END OF PART 1 TENTATIVE RULING, SEE PART 2) = (302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: LAURA WOODWARD VS. LEGACY PROBATE INTERNATIONAL, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Responses By Defendant
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Wednesday, January 22, 2025, line 2, PLAINTIFF LAURA WOODWARD'S Motion To Compel Further Responses By Defendant (tentative ruling part 1, see part 2) Defendant's motion to compel further production is granted in part. The motion is granted in part as to Request 3. The cited discovery meets the standard for discovery relevance. The Kraus declaration shows that as presently phrased the request is unduly burdensome. (Kraus Decl., pars. 4-6.) Therefore, documents "referencing or related to" the specified estates do not include i) communications and documents where defendant attempted to locate possible estate heirs who could share in distribution of the estate's assets; ii) genealogy documents showing possible heirs and their contact information; iii) documents and correspondence with public agencies that keep vital statistics records for the purpose of locating heirs and proving heirships that can share in the estate; iv) proof of heirship document (such as birth, marriage and death records); v) defendant's communications with the estate beneficiaries, including requests for updates and other information regarding the case. If there are any responsive documents not produced on the grounds of the attorney-client privilege or work product doctrine, defendant shall provide a privilege log. (CCP 2031.240(c).) Defendant shall provide code compliant responses by February 10, 2025. Grant as to Request 4. The documents referencing Applegate and his firms are reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence. (CCP 2031.240(c).) If there are any responsive documents not produced on the grounds of the attorney-client privilege or work product doctrine, defendant shall provide a privilege log. (CCP 2031.240(c).) Defendant shall provide code compliant responses by February 10, 2025. The request for sanctions is denied because both parties acted with substantial justification. (END OF TENTATIVE RULING PART 1, SEE PART 2) = (302/HEK)

rulings.Case Title: VIRGINIA JONES VS. MERCURY INSURANCE SERVICES, LLC, A CALIFORNIA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Attorney'S Fees, Declaration Of John Scott Carter In Support Thereof; [Proposed] Order
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Wednesday, January 22, 2025, line 12, DEFENDANT GARY BEMIS' Motion For Attorney'S Fees Defendant Gary Bemis' motion for attorney's fees is granted in part. Mr. Bemis is awarded $6,000 in reasonable fees for his counsel's work on his successful anti-SLAPP motion. The award is calculated based on ten hours or attorney time at $450 per hour and 10 hours of paralegal time at $150 per hour. Plaintiff Virginia Jones' request for sanctions in the form of a reduction off the award to Mr. Bemis is denied. Ms. Jones has not shown that Mr. Bemis acted frivolously or for the purpose of delay. rulings.Case Title: LCAO TECH LLC, A DELAWARE LIMITED LIABILITY VS. QUIMBIK, INC., A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Wednesday, January 22, 2025, line 8, DEFENDANT QUIMBIK, INC., A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION'S DEMURRER to 1ST Amended COMPLAINT Defendant Quimbik, Inc.'s demurrer to the sole cause of action for breach of contract in the first amended complaint is overruled. The first amended complaint does not show on its face that plaintiff LCAO Tech LLC's breach of contract claim is time-barred. The allegations that Quimbik continued to work to finalize, or at least committed to do work to finalize, the websites and mobile application in 2021 and thereafter (e.g. FAC pars. 85-89, 93-94, 102-103, 108, 162) and never provided the contractually required acceptance document to establish the completion of its contract obligations (FAC pars. 25-26, 119-121) sufficiently allege that the contract remained executory and the breaches complained of (FAC pars. 163-166) occurred later than four years prior to the filing of this lawsuit. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) rulings.Case Title: JULIO C. PAZ GALLO VS. AMERICAN HONDA MOTOR CO., INC., A CALIFORNIA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Compel The Deposition Of Defendants Person Most Knowledgeable And Production Of Documents
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery Calendar for Wednesday, January 22, 2025, line 5 PLAINTIFF JULIO PAZ GALLO'S Motion To Compel The Deposition Of Defendants Person Most Knowledgeable And Production Of Documents Plaintiff Julio Gallo's motion to compel PMK depositions and production of documents is granted. Defendant American Honda must provide at least two dates prior to May 31, 2025 that each of its three corporate designees is available to be deposed and must produce all responsive documents other than those claimed to be privileged no later than 14 calendar days before the agreed-upon date for the first deposition. All responsive documents withheld on the grounds of privilege must be included in a privilege log to be served at the time the responsive non-privileged documents are produced. The parties' inability to agree on deposition dates without court intervention is not commendable. Mr. Gallo has shown that all documents he seeks meet the standard for discovery relevance and American Honda has not provided any evidentiary support that producing those documents is an undue burden on it. Because American Honda acted with substantial justification, Mr. Gallo's requests for monetary sanctions is denied. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) rulings.Case Title: PATRINA HARRISON VS. WHOLE FOODS MARKET CALIFORNIA INC.
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER to Amended COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Wednesday, January 22, 2025, line 9, 1 - DEFENDANT WHOLE FOODS MARKET CALIFORNIA INC's DEMURRER to 1ST Amended COMPLAINT Defendant Whole Food Market California, Inc.'s demurrer to the first through fifth, seventh through ninth and twelfth causes of action in the first amended complaint is overruled as to the first, second, fourth, fifth, seventh, eighth, ninth and twelfth causes of action and sustained without leave to amend as to the third cause of action. The parties' requests to take judicial notice of the contents of the police report are denied. The contents of the police report are not admissible because the police report contains multiple levels of inadmissible hearsay. Nor are the contents of the police report judicially noticeable since the police report includes matters that are reasonably disputable and not easily verifiable. Since the contents of the police report are not judicially noticeable, all grounds for the demurrer relying on the contents of the police report lack merit. Moreover, plaintiff Patricia Harrison adequately alleges that she was threatened and harmed because of her race, thereby defeating the additional grounds for the demurrer to the fourth, fifth, and seventh causes of action. Because Ms. Harrison adequately alleges pre-suit notice, the first ground for the demurrer to the eighth cause of action lacks merit. Because Ms. Harrison adequately alleges conduct prohibited by the CLRA and UCL, the second ground for the demurrer to the eighth cause of action and the sole ground for the demurrer to the ninth cause of action lack merit. Because respondeat superior is not a cause of action, the demurrer to the third cause of action is sustained without leave to amend. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. rulings.Case Title: PATRICK BLAKE VS. TURO, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO COMPEL ARBITRATION
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Wednesday, January 22, 2025, line 13, DEFENDANT TURO INC. MOTION TO COMPEL ARBITRATION (tentative ruling part 1 of 2) Defendant Turo, Inc.'s motion to compel arbitration and stay proceedings is granted. Although not filed with permission of the court as required, the first amended complaint (FAC) is deemed the operative complaint because, if plaintiff Patrick Blake had sought permission to file the FAC, he would have been granted permission to do so. All causes of action in the FAC except those seeking public injunctive relief must be arbitrated per the parties' arbitration agreement. The causes of action seeking public injunctive relief are the sixth (CLRA), seventh (UCL), eleventh (failure to maintain records), and sixteenth (CPRA violations). This lawsuit, including the causes of action seeking public injunctive relief, is stayed pending conclusion of the arbitration on the arbitrable claims. Mr. Blake has not shown that his desire to take discovery warrants a continuance of the hearing on Turo's motion to compel arbitration. All the issues on this motion can be fairly decided now based on the information available to the parties. The parties' arbitration agreement is valid and enforceable as to all of Mr. Blake's claims except the claims seeking public injunctive relief and is not unconscionable. The broad language of the arbitration agreement covers all of Mr. Blake's claims except the claims seeking public injunctive relief. Mr. Blake has not shown that the arbitration agreement is either procedurally or substantively unconscionable. It is not procedurally unconscionable because it has an opt-out provision, which Mr. Blake failed to avail himself of. The opt-out provision shows that the agreement was not imposed on Mr. Blake on a take-it-or-leave-it basis. Mr. Blake's only assertion of substantive unconscionability is his claimed inability to pay arbitration costs, yet Mr. Blake fails to provide any evidentiary support for that assertion. (End of tentative ruling part 1 see part 2) = (302/HEK)
